title: “And how, good sirs, is one not defiled by sensual lust? Here, good sirs, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he is…
content: “And how, good sirs, is one not defiled by sensual lust? Here, good sirs, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he is not attached to agreeable forms nor repelled by disagreeable forms. He dwells with bodily mindfulness established, with an immeasurable mind. He knows as it actually is the liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom. Here, evil, unwholesome states that have arisen are abandoned without remainder. When he tastes a flavor with the tongue … When he cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, he is not attached to agreeable phenomena nor repelled by disagreeable phenomena. He dwells with bodily mindfulness established, with an immeasurable mind. He knows as it actually is the liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom. Here, evil, unwholesome states that have arisen are abandoned without remainder. This, good sirs, is called a monk who is not defiled by sensual lust regarding forms … regarding tastes … regarding phenomena. A monk living thus, good sirs, if Māra approaches him through the eye, Māra does not gain an opportunity, Māra does not gain an object. If Māra approaches him through the tongue … through the mind, Māra does not gain an opportunity, Māra does not gain an object.”

title: “Monks, on King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches where neither monkeys nor humans go. On King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches…
content: “Monks, on King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches where neither monkeys nor humans go. On King Snowy Mountain there are rough, uneven stretches where monkeys go but humans do not. On King Snowy Mountain there are even, pleasant stretches where both monkeys and humans go. There, monks, hunters set a trap of sticky resin on the paths where monkeys go, to catch monkeys. Here, monks, those monkeys that are not foolish and not greedy avoid the trap of resin when they see it. But a foolish, greedy monkey, seeing the resin, reaches out with its hand and gets stuck. Wanting to free its hand, it reaches out with the other hand and gets stuck. Wanting to free both hands, it reaches out with one foot and gets stuck. Wanting to free its hands and foot, it reaches out with the other foot and gets stuck. Wanting to free its hands and feet, it puts its mouth to it and gets stuck. Thus, monks, the monkey, caught in five places, lies down screeching, having fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the hunter’s will. The hunter then kills it, roasts it over a charcoal fire, and goes away as he wishes. So too, monks, for one who wanders outside his own proper range, into the domain of others. For one who wanders outside his proper range, into the domain of others, Māra gains an opportunity, Māra gains an object.”

title: “Furthermore, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Whatever sensual pleasures present and future, whatever perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future, whatever forms present and future…
content: “Furthermore, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Whatever sensual pleasures present and future, whatever perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future, whatever forms present and future — all are the four great elements and form derived from the four great elements.’ While he practices thus, dwells often thus, his mind becomes serene in that element. With serene mind, he attains the Imperturbable (āneñja) either in this very life or he becomes strongly inclined to wisdom. At the breakup of the body, after death, it is possible that his consciousness, proceeding accordingly, may attain the Imperturbable.”

title: Elder Nisabha: Having abandoned the five strands of sensual pleasure, Those things so agreeable, Which delight the mind — With faith, I went forth, Putting…
content:
Elder Nisabha:

Having abandoned the five strands of sensual pleasure,
Those things so agreeable,
Which delight the mind —
With faith, I went forth,
Putting an end to suffering.

I do not delight in death,
I do not delight in life.
I await the time,
Mindful and clearly comprehending.

title: “What leads the world along? What torments itself? And what is the one thing Upon which everything depends?” “Craving leads the world along, Craving torments…
content:
“What leads the world along?
What torments itself?
And what is the one thing
Upon which everything depends?”

“Craving leads the world along,
Craving torments itself,
Craving is the one thing
Upon which everything depends.”

title: Elder Kappa: Full of various impurities, Where there is no purity — Like a long-stagnant puddle, A great boil, a wound. Full of blood and…
content:
Elder Kappa:

Full of various impurities,
Where there is no purity —
Like a long-stagnant puddle,
A great boil, a wound.

Full of blood and pus,
Sunk in a pit of filth —
This body oozes fluid,
Constantly oozing impurity.

Bound by sixty sinews,
Plastered with flesh as mortar,
Clad in skin as armor —
This foul body is useless.

Connected by a chain of bones,
Tied together with sinews —
By their combined power,
They create various postures.

Always heading toward death,
Approaching the end of life —
There, abandoned,
People go according to their desire.

The body, shrouded by ignorance,
Bound by four bonds,
Sinks in the flood,
Caught in the net of underlying tendencies —

Fettered by five hindrances,
Obsessed by thought,
Following the root of craving,
Covered by the veil of delusion.

Thus this body wanders,
Led by the chariot of kamma —
Sometimes success, sometimes failure —
Enduring many births of craving.

One who thinks ‘this body is mine’
Is a foolish worldling,
Increasing the fearful cemetery,
Clinging tightly to renewed existence.

One who avoids this body
As one avoids a snake smeared with feces —
That one abandons the root of existence
And attains Nibbāna, free from defilements.

title: One whose mind inclines to renunciation, His heart tends toward seclusion; One whose mind inclines to non-ill-will, His heart tends toward the cessation of clinging;…
content:
One whose mind inclines to renunciation,
His heart tends toward seclusion;
One whose mind inclines to non-ill-will,
His heart tends toward the cessation of clinging;
One whose mind inclines to the cessation of craving,
His heart tends toward the absence of delusion;
Seeing the place of arising,
His mind is rightly liberated.
For one rightly liberated,
The monk with a pure mind,
There is nothing further to do,
Nothing remains to be done.
Like a solid mass of rock,
Unmoved by the wind,
So too, all forms,
Tastes, sounds, smells, contacts,
And all phenomena —
Agreeable or disagreeable —
Cannot disturb one such as this,
Whose mind is steady, liberated,
Seeing the nature of their cessation.

title: “Furthermore, wanderers, a brahmin says: ‘All sensual pleasures are impermanent, suffering, subject to change.’ That brahmin speaks truth, not falsehood. And by directly knowing the…
content: “Furthermore, wanderers, a brahmin says: ‘All sensual pleasures are impermanent, suffering, subject to change.’ That brahmin speaks truth, not falsehood. And by directly knowing the truth contained therein, he practices disenchantment, dispassion, and cessation regarding sensual pleasures.”

title: “Monks, have you seen a painting called ‘The Wandering One’?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “Monks, that painting was created by the mind. Yet the mind is…
content: “Monks, have you seen a painting called ‘The Wandering One’?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “Monks, that painting was created by the mind. Yet the mind is even more diverse than that painting. Therefore, monks, one should constantly reflect on one’s own mind thus: ‘For a long time, this mind has been defiled by greed, hatred, and delusion. Through the defilement of the mind, beings are defiled. Through the purification of the mind, beings are purified.’”

title: “I do not see any other form, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a man as the form of a woman. The…
content: “I do not see any other form, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a man as the form of a woman. The form of a woman, monks, obsesses and occupies the mind of a man.”
